items.document:
I'm starting a public experiment where I'm handing my entire codebase over to AI. No humans allowed to write or review a single line of code!

It's called a Dark Factory. And no, I wouldn't recommend it for reliable production code - yet. That's exactly why I'm calling it an experiment.

Dan Shapiro laid out five levels of AI coding autonomy earlier this year, using a self-driving car analogy. Level 3 is where reliable production AI coding lives right now: AI generates most of the code, but you're still reviewing every plan and every PR before it ships. That's what I teach in my Agentic Coding Course. You're in a self-driving car but still watching the road and ready to course correct if needed.

Level 5 is the Dark Factory - no steering wheel. Nothing gets reviewed. Even I'm a little uncomfortable with it! But this is where AI coding is heading, and nobody has run it as an open experiment before.

StrongDM did a version of this internally (32K lines shipped, zero human review, went viral in February), but you can't watch it work or participate. That's the gap I'm filling!

Here's how I'm building it, and this is where Archon (my open source harness builder) becomes the backbone.

Governance layer:

Three files loaded at the start of every workflow: MISSION.md (what the factory can build, what it must never touch), FACTORY_RULES.md (triage, PR size limits, quality gates, mandatory browser testing), CLAUDE.md (stack, layout, conventions). This is the only meaningful way I can stay aligned with a system that ships without asking me.

Archon workflows:
 
Archon is my open source harness builder - it turns your coding process into packaged up YAML workflows that can include both AI and deterministic steps. Three of them run the factory:

- Triage: classifies GitHub issues against MISSION.md + FACTORY_RULES.md
- Implement: runs in parallel for every accepted issue
- Validate: completely separate workflow to fully review PRs + merge

The validate workflow uses the "holdout" pattern from StrongDM. The validator only sees the user journey and the diff, zero context from development. No sycophancy, no rewriting tests to fake green.

If validation passes, it merges to main. No human approval at all.

The coding model is Minimax M2.7 routed into Claude Code via env vars. Purely an economics call, my Anthropic subscription would hit rate limits in hours at this throughput!

The app the factory is building is an AI Tutor, a RAG agent over my YouTube content. That part I'm genuinely excited about on its own.

Within a week or two it'll be deployed! You'll be able to file real GitHub issues and watch the factory accept, implement, validate, and ship them without me touching a line. Full architecture breakdown is on my YouTube channel!

Cole Medin is launching a public experiment called 'Dark Factory' where AI generates and deploys code with zero human review - a Level 5 autonomous coding system. He's building it with three governance files and three Archon workflows (Triage, Implement, Validate) that use a 'holdout pattern' to prevent validator bias. The system uses Minimax M2.7 routed through Claude Code and will build an AI Tutor RAG agent, deploying within weeks as an open experiment allowing public GitHub issue participation.

directClaims: Starting a public experiment handing entire codebase to AI with no human code review, Dan Shapiro laid out five levels of AI coding autonomy using self-driving car analogy, Level 3 is where reliable production AI coding lives right now, Level 5 (Dark Factory) has no steering wheel and nothing gets reviewed, StrongDM shipped 32K lines with zero human review in February, Three governance files: MISSION.md, FACTORY_RULES.md, CLAUDE.md, Three Archon workflows run the factory: Triage, Implement, Validate, Validate workflow uses the holdout pattern from StrongDM, Coding model is Minimax M2.7 routed into Claude Code, The factory is building an AI Tutor RAG agent over YouTube content, Will be deployed within a week or two

inferredBeliefs: AI coding autonomy is progressing toward full automation, Current human review processes limit AI coding potential, Open experimentation with AI coding can benefit the community, Governance frameworks are essential for autonomous AI systems, Validator separation prevents validator bias and test manipulation, AI coding will eventually reach production-grade reliability without human oversight, Economic constraints (rate limits) drive tool selection decisions

Paste any GitHub URL and get a full interactive architecture map in seconds.

CodeFlow is a single HTML file that maps any GitHub repo's architecture.

Paste a URL or upload a project, get a full interactive dependency graph. 

No install, no account, no build step. 

Everything runs in your browser. Your code never touches a server.

Pick any file and see its blast radius. 

That means every other file that would break if you changed it.

It also scans for security issues automatically. 

Hardcoded API keys, SQL injection patterns, eval() calls, debug statements in production.

Here's what else it detects:

> Singleton, factory, and observer patterns
> Code ownership per file from git history
> Circular dependencies and dead code
> Commit frequency heatmaps across files

The whole thing ships as one HTML file. Clone and open index.html. 

Works with private repos too.

Fully open source. 

Link in comments.
↓
Check out AlphaSignal.ai to get a daily summary of top models, repos, and papers in AI. Read by 280,000+ devs.

Eric Vyacheslav presents CodeFlow, a browser-based tool for analyzing GitHub repository architecture without requiring installation or server uploads. It provides interactive dependency graphs, security issue detection, code pattern identification, and git-based code ownership analysis, all delivered as a single HTML file that's open source and privacy-preserving.

items.document:
CoT, ReAct, ToT, RAG, MCP, DAG.

Most engineers building agents can't explain what these mean. 

They copy-paste from tutorials and wonder why their agent loops forever.

Here's the AI Agent stack, decoded:

🔹 CoT (Chain of Thought)
Make the model think step by step before answering.
Without CoT, LLMs jump to conclusions. With it, they reason through problems.
The difference between "guess" and "derive."

🔹 ReAct (Reason + Act)
CoT plus tool use. The model reasons, then acts, then observes, then reasons again.
This is how agents actually work. Think → Do → See → Repeat.
Most "agents" skip the reasoning. That's why they fail.

🔹 ToT (Tree of Thoughts)
CoT explores one path. ToT explores many.
The model branches, evaluates, backtracks. Slower, but catches mistakes CoT misses.
Use it when the first answer is usually wrong.

🔹 RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation)
Don't make the model memorize everything. Fetch what it needs at runtime.
Vector search → relevant context → grounded answer.
Without RAG, your agent hallucinates. With it, your agent cites sources.

🔹 MCP (Model Context Protocol)
Anthropic's standard for connecting LLMs to external tools.
One protocol. Any tool. No custom integrations per service.
The USB-C of AI agents.

🔹 DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph)
How you orchestrate multi-step workflows without infinite loops.
Task A → Task B → Task C. No cycles. Clear dependencies.
LangGraph, Prefect, Airflow — all DAGs under the hood.

The engineers who understand these don't just USE agent frameworks.

They understand WHY agents work.

Want to go deeper? 

Full Single-Agent Patterns deep-dive  in comments 👇

💾 Bookmark this before your next agent breaks and you don't know why.

Paolo Perrone explains six fundamental concepts in the AI agent stack (CoT, ReAct, ToT, RAG, MCP, DAG), arguing that most engineers use these patterns without understanding how they work. He provides concise definitions emphasizing that understanding these patterns is crucial for building effective agents and avoiding common failure modes.

directClaims: Most engineers building agents can't explain what CoT, ReAct, ToT, RAG, MCP, and DAG mean, Without CoT, LLMs jump to conclusions, ReAct is CoT plus tool use with reasoning, acting, and observing cycles, ToT explores multiple paths while CoT explores one path, RAG prevents hallucinations by fetching relevant context at runtime, MCP is Anthropic's standard for connecting LLMs to external tools, DAG orchestrates multi-step workflows without infinite loops, LangGraph, Prefect, and Airflow are all DAGs under the hood

inferredBeliefs: Understanding the underlying patterns is more valuable than just using frameworks, Agent failures are often due to skipping reasoning steps, Engineers should understand the theory behind agent architectures, Proper agent design requires knowledge of multiple complementary techniques

mentionedTools: LangGraph, Prefect, Airflow, MCP (Model Context Protocol)

𝐂𝐥𝐚𝐮𝐝𝐞 𝐂𝐨𝐝𝐞 𝐏𝐫𝐨𝐣𝐞𝐜𝐭 𝐒𝐭𝐫𝐮𝐜𝐭𝐮𝐫𝐞: 𝐄𝐯𝐞𝐫𝐲 𝐅𝐢𝐥𝐞 𝐚𝐧𝐝 𝐅𝐨𝐥𝐝𝐞𝐫 𝐄𝐱𝐩𝐥𝐚𝐢𝐧𝐞𝐝

Most Claude Code projects start with a CLAUDE.md and nothing else.
Here is the full project structure that turns Claude from a coding assistant into an engineering partner.

𝐓𝐇𝐄 𝐂𝐎𝐌𝐏𝐋𝐄𝐓𝐄 𝐃𝐈𝐑𝐄𝐂𝐓𝐎𝐑𝐘

your-project/

1. CLAUDE.md
Loaded at session start. 
Defines project overview, tech stack, and commands. 
Contains coding conventions and architecture. 
Supports CLAUDE.local.md overrides for personal preferences without affecting the team.

2. CLAUDE.local.md
Your personal overrides. 
Keeps individual preferences separate from shared project context.

3. mcp.json
Stores MCP integration configs. 
Connects to GitHub, JIRA, Slack, databases. 
Shared across the team via git. This single file controls every external tool your agent can reach.

4. claude/settings.json
Controls permissions and tool access. 
Defines model selection and hooks. 
Supports settings.local.json overrides for individual developers.

5. claude/rules/
Modular .md files organized by topic. 
Covers style, testing, and API design. Can target specific files or paths.

• code-style.md: How code should look
• testing.md: Testing standards and patterns
• api-conventions.md: API design rules

Rules load contextually. Claude follows code-style.md when writing code, testing.md when generating tests.

6. claude/commands/
Custom slash commands (/project:name). 
Used for repeatable workflows. Supports shell execution.

• review.md: Code review workflow
• fix-issue.md: Issue resolution steps

Type /project:review and Claude runs your entire review process.

7. claude/skills/
Auto-triggered based on task context. 
Loads only when needed. 
Keeps context lightweight.

• deploy/SKILL.md: Deployment procedures
• deploy/deploy-config.md: Configuration details

Skills activate automatically when Claude detects a relevant task.

8. claude/agents/
Specialized sub-agents with roles. 
Isolated context windows. 
Custom tools and model preferences.

• code-reviewer.md: Dedicated code review agent
• security-auditor.md: Security-focused analysis agent

Each agent operates in its own context without polluting the main conversation.

9. claude/hooks/
Event-driven scripts (pre/post tool use). 
Automates validation, linting, and formatting. 
Blocks unsafe operations.

• validate-bash.sh: Validates bash commands before execution

Hooks are your guardrails. They run automatically before or after Claude takes action.

The quality of Claude Code's output is directly proportional to the quality of your project structure. 

Invest in the structure once and every session benefits.

𝐖𝐡𝐚𝐭 𝐝𝐨𝐞𝐬 𝐲𝐨𝐮𝐫 𝐂𝐥𝐚𝐮𝐝𝐞 𝐂𝐨𝐝𝐞 𝐩𝐫𝐨𝐣𝐞𝐜𝐭 𝐬𝐭𝐫𝐮𝐜𝐭𝐮𝐫𝐞 𝐥𝐨𝐨𝐤 𝐥𝐢𝐤𝐞 𝐭𝐨𝐝𝐚𝐲?

♻️ Repost this to help your network get started
➕ Follow Sandipan Bhaumik for more

AI agents fail without strong memory layers.

5 key AI memory systems, explained simply.

1. 𝗦𝗵𝗼𝗿𝘁-𝗧𝗲𝗿𝗺 𝗠𝗲𝗺𝗼𝗿𝘆 (𝗥𝗲𝗮𝗹-𝗧𝗶𝗺𝗲 𝗖𝗼𝗻𝘁𝗲𝘅𝘁)

𝗜𝗻𝗽𝘂𝘁 𝗣𝗿𝗼𝗰𝗲𝘀𝘀𝗶𝗻𝗴

• Understand intent

𝗖𝗼𝗻𝘁𝗲𝘅𝘁 𝗠𝗮𝗻𝗮𝗴𝗲𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁

𝗕𝘂𝗳𝗳𝗲𝗿 𝗛𝗮𝗻𝗱𝗹𝗶𝗻𝗴

2. 𝗟𝗼𝗻𝗴-𝗧𝗲𝗿𝗺 𝗠𝗲𝗺𝗼𝗿𝘆 (𝗣𝗲𝗿𝘀𝗶𝘀𝘁𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝗞𝗻𝗼𝘄𝗹𝗲𝗱𝗴𝗲)

𝗟𝗲𝗮𝗿𝗻𝗶𝗻𝗴

The future of AI systems depends on combining all these memory layers together.

He argues that AI agent success depends on integrating all these memory layers together.

items.document:
4 habits that make Claude Code more reliable in practice:


1---- Don’t turn CLAUDE. md into a giant instruction dump

Use it for stable things: project context, architecture notes, conventions, important commands.

If something is really a repeatable procedure, checklist, or playbook, it usually belongs somewhere else.


2---- Use Plan Mode before bigger changes

For multi-file edits, refactors, or anything slightly messy, it is usually safer to let Claude inspect and plan first before touching code.


3---- Turn repeated prompts into skills

If you keep typing the same “review this,” “debug this,” or “prepare this for deploy” instructions, that is usually a sign the workflow should become reusable instead of rewritten every time.


4---- Use hooks for things that should happen every time

Formatting, validation, guardrails, notifications - anything deterministic is better handled by the system than by hoping the model remembers.


The more useful shift, to me, is moving the right things out of the prompt and into the environment.

That’s where repeated prompting starts turning into structure.

If you want to learn Claude Code more deeply, we’re also running a live course and hands-on workshop on it:

𝐂𝐥𝐚𝐮𝐝𝐞 𝐂𝐨𝐝𝐞 𝐌𝐚𝐬𝐭𝐞𝐫𝐲
🔗https://lnkd.in/gu5C-vKq

*There’s also a 10-day satisfaction guarantee if you want to take a closer look!

Alex Wang shares four habits for improving Claude Code reliability: avoiding cluttered documentation, using Plan Mode for complex changes, converting repeated prompts into reusable skills, and implementing hooks for deterministic tasks. The core principle is shifting from prompt-based instructions to structured environmental setup.

This GitHub repo just hit 16k stars 🔥 It gives your AI agent eyes to see the entire internet:

Most agents are blind to the live web. They can reason and write code, but ask them to check a tweet or pull a YouTube transcript - they're stuck.

Agent-Reach fixes this. It's a Python CLI that connects your agent to the platforms where information actually lives:

• X
• Reddit
• YouTube
• GitHub
• Any webpage or RSS feed

No paid APIs. No subscriptions. Setup is one line.

It auto-detects your environment and tells you what's ready. Run `agent-reach doctor` anytime to see what's working and what's not.

Works with Claude Code, Cursor, Windsurf - any agent that runs commands.

Link to the project: https://lnkd.in/dv-vvezp

--
♻️ Find it useful? Leave a reaction and share with your network!

items.document:
One of the best Claude Code repos I’ve come across recently.

Not just because of how much is inside it, but because of how complete the system feels.

What I like most is how it brings together configuration, workflows, memory, security, and governance into one setup. 
Among many strong Claude Code and agent workflow repos, that still feels relatively rare.

It also covers a pretty broad surface: skills, memory optimization, continuous learning, security scanning, hooks, rules, and MCP configs.


What I find especially interesting about it:

1---- It makes it very clear that a lot of the improvement in AI coding is not only in the model itself, but also in the operating layer around the model.

You can see that in the way this repo emphasizes agents, skills, hooks, continuous learning, and security scanning as part of the system design.


2---- It has strong reference value, even if you never install the whole repo.

You can look at how it organizes subagents, turns workflows into reusable skills, and builds memory, security, and maintenance layers into the system. That alone makes it worth studying.


3---- It is already moving beyond a single-tool setup, toward cross-tool compatibility and a more general agent harness layer.

Overall, a very valuable repo to learn from and experiment with.

Repo created by Affaan Mustafa.
I’ll drop the link in the comments.


If you're into AI, join our newly launched AI Academy for free early access - available to the first 1,000 who join.
https://bit.ly/3OeNi2y

Building an agent is only part of the job.

The more important part is everything around it:

- API connections
- webhooks, auth, and data pipelines
- frontend/backend integration
- the security and permissions layer

And you still need traditional software engineering, plus product thinking - like how users interact with agents.

So yes, building agents matters, and it’s fun.
But a big part of the real work is putting all the pieces together in a way that’s actually reliable.

If you’re new to this, this free 2-day AI Mastermind from Outskill could be a good place to start.

Link here https://lnkd.in/gd3PaXK2

Alex Wang emphasizes that successful AI agent deployment requires much more than building the core agent itself. The critical work involves integrating APIs, managing authentication and data pipelines, frontend/backend integration, implementing security layers, and applying traditional software engineering practices with product-centric thinking. He positions reliable system architecture and integration as the real challenge in agent development.

directClaims: Building an agent is only part of the job, API connections, webhooks, auth, and data pipelines are critical components, Frontend/backend integration is necessary for agent deployment, Security and permissions layer is essential, Traditional software engineering is needed alongside agent building, Product thinking about user interactions with agents is important, The real work involves putting all pieces together reliably

items.document:
🚨 Andrej Karpathy’s just shared his new set-up on 𝕏.

It’s literally a self-improving second brain… with zero manual editing 🤯

A living AI knowledge base that continuously fixes and refines itself.

Let me break it down.

Instead of relying on complex RAG, the LLM pulls raw research directly into an Obsidian Markdown wiki. 

It completely takes over:

✦ Index creation
✦ System linting
✦ Native Q&A routing

The core process is beautifully simple:

→ You dump raw sources into a folder
→ The LLM auto-compiles an indexed .md wiki
→ You ask complex questions
→ It generates outputs (Marp slides, matplotlib plots) and files them back in

The big-picture implication of this is just wild.

When agents maintain their own memory layer, they don’t need massive, expensive context limits.

They really just need two things:

→ Clean file organization
→ The ability to query their own indexes

Forget stuffing everything into one giant prompt.

This approach is way cheaper, highly scalable... and 100% inspectable!

_____

1. You can find this self-improving wiki approach from Karpathy here:
→ https://lnkd.in/eUR3fEUE

2. Here's a great breakdown of Karpathy’s framework, walking through every stage from ingestion to what comes next:

items.document:
I built an AI second brain with Claude Code over the last three months. It saves me 12+ hours a week! But the part that actually changed how I work isn't the time savings.

It's that the system anticipates what I need before I think to ask.

Every conversation I have with it, every decision I make, every email it reads on my behalf - it all feeds into a memory layer that gets sharper over time. It learns how I communicate, what I prioritize, which tasks tend to slip. Three months in, it knows my patterns well enough to flag things I'd miss on my own!

The proactive piece (inspired by OpenClaw) is what makes it feel different from every other AI tool I've used. It runs in the background, monitoring my calendar, email, tasks, and Slack. But it doesn't just alert me. It acts. It drafts replies to emails I haven't gotten to, handles routine tasks in Asana, catches scheduling conflicts before they become problems. When something needs my judgment, it surfaces it. When it can handle something on its own, it just does.

I don't open the system and ask questions. The system is already working before I sit down.

And because the whole thing compounds, the actions get smarter. It's not matching against static rules. It's reasoning with context about my work, my priorities, how things went last time. The decisions it makes today are meaningfully better than the ones it made even a few weeks ago.

That's the gap between a tool you use and a system that works for you. The tool sits there until you remember it exists. The system is already thinking about your day.

I just posted a full breakdown on YouTube walking through every component of the second brain, how the proactive layer works, and what three months of building this looks like end to end.

And this Friday (April 4 at 9:00 AM CST) I'm running a live workshop in the Dynamous community (link in my bio) where you build the whole thing from scratch. Eight modules, starting from zero, leaving with a working second brain on your machine!

Cole Medin describes building an AI second brain using Claude Code that delivers value not through time savings alone, but through proactive, context-aware assistance that anticipates user needs. The system continuously learns from user behavior and operates autonomously in the background, handling tasks in email, Asana, and Slack while escalating judgment calls. He emphasizes the philosophical difference between reactive tools and proactive systems, and is promoting educational content including a YouTube breakdown and live workshop.

directClaims: Saves 12+ hours a week, System anticipates needs before being asked, Memory layer gets sharper over time, Learns communication patterns and priorities, Can flag things the user would miss, Runs in background monitoring calendar, email, tasks, and Slack, Drafts email replies and handles routine tasks in Asana, Catches scheduling conflicts proactively, Actions get smarter over time through compounding, Reasons with context rather than matching static rules

inferredBeliefs: Proactive AI assistance is superior to reactive tools, Continuous learning from user behavior creates competitive advantage, AI that operates autonomously when possible is more valuable than tools requiring explicit requests, Context-aware decision making improves with time and data, The difference between a tool and a system is fundamental to user value

items.document:
The biggest problem in AI coding right now is sycophancy.

Not in the broad "AI agrees with everything you say" sense, though that's real. Specifically this: when you have a coding agent evaluate its own work, it's a student grading its own homework. It'll flag a couple of minor issues to make the review look credible, but the deeper problems get stuffed under the rug.

The model was trained to agree. That bias doesn't disappear just because you asked it to be critical!

So the fix is architecture, not "prompting harder".

I built what I call an adversarial dev harness, inspired by how Anthropic is thinking about long-running coding agents. The core idea comes from GANs (generative adversarial networks), where a generator and a discriminator are trained against each other. Here, the generator writes code. The evaluator's sole job is to find flaws, not approve.

The architecture looks like this:

User prompt goes to a Planner, which expands it into a full spec. Then you enter a sprint cycle. Before a single line of code is written, the Generator and Evaluator negotiate the evaluation criteria together. They agree up front on what each sprint looks like and how it gets scored on a 1-10 scale. Only then does the Generator implement, and the Evaluator rips it apart against those pre-negotiated criteria.

Two core principles at work here:

1. Separate evaluation from coding entirely (different agents, isolated contexts, no shared bias)
2. Scope and contract before implementation (criteria set before code is written, not after)

The results are legitimately incredible. I used this harness to one-shot a full-stack RAG app with a polished chat UI, token streaming, source citations, the whole thing. Ran for about four hours. And I used Claude Sonnet 4.6 for it, not Opus (huge difference in capability)!

That's the real leverage: a better harness lets you use a cheaper, faster model and still get better output than a stronger model running alone.

I posted a full breakdown on YouTube (link to my channel in my bio) with code walkthroughs for both the Claude Code and Codex versions of the harness, plus a live demo of the RAG app it built.

Cole Medin argues that sycophancy is the core problem in AI coding, where agents fail to critically evaluate their own work due to training bias. He proposes an adversarial dev harness architecture inspired by GANs that separates code generation from evaluation using different agents and pre-negotiates success criteria before implementation. He claims this approach enables cheaper models like Claude Sonnet 4.6 to produce superior results compared to stronger models running alone, demonstrating it by building a full-stack RAG application with polished UI in four hours.

directClaims: The biggest problem in AI coding right now is sycophancy, When a coding agent evaluates its own work, it flags minor issues but misses deeper problems, Models trained to agree have that bias persist even when asked to be critical, The fix is architecture, not prompting harder, Separate evaluation from coding entirely with different agents and isolated contexts prevents shared bias, Setting evaluation criteria before implementation produces better results than setting them after, An adversarial dev harness using Claude Sonnet 4.6 successfully built a full-stack RAG app with polished UI, token streaming, and source citations in four hours, A better harness lets you use a cheaper, faster model and still get better output than a stronger model running alone

inferredBeliefs: Current self-evaluation mechanisms in AI coding agents are insufficient, Architectural changes are more effective than prompt engineering for solving AI limitations, Adversarial approaches borrowed from GANs can improve coding agent quality, Pre-negotiation of success criteria reduces bias in code evaluation, Claude Sonnet 4.6 is significantly more capable than expected for complex coding tasks, System design and harness architecture are key leverage points for AI capability

items.document:
Someone just open-sourced the "Docker for AI agents" 🤯

Define your agent once. Run it in Claude Code, OpenAI, CrewAI, LangChain, Google Gemini CLI, and OpenClaw.

Meet GitAgent. Your repo is the agent.

Right now every framework defines agents differently. Your CrewAI agent can't run in Claude Code. Your LangChain agent can't port to OpenAI. 

You want to switch frameworks? You rewrite everything. Prompts, tools, memory, config. All of it. Again.

That's not a feature of the ecosystem. That's vendor lock-in wearing an open-source badge.

GitAgent replaces all of it with a standard folder structure.

agent.yaml for config. SOUL.md for personality. RULES.md for hard constraints. skills/ for capabilities. tools/ for integrations. memory/ for persistence.

Two files are required. Everything else is optional.

Then one command exports to any runtime.

Same repo runs in Claude Code, OpenAI Agents SDK, CrewAI, LangGraph, Google Gemini CLI, OpenClaw, and Cursor. 

No rewrite. No adapter code. No framework-specific config.

Git becomes the supervision layer. Every prompt change is a commit. Every rollback is a checkout. Every agent review is a PR. Agent quality gets treated like code quality.

What makes this more than a packaging spec:
• Built-in segregation of duties for regulated industries. Define maker, checker, and auditor roles. 
• Sub-agents compose like submodules. Fork a public agent, swap its SOUL.md, plug in your own tools.
• CI/CD runs gitagent validate on every push. Bad agent configs don't ship.

Dockerfiles didn't replace container runtimes. They gave developers one format that every runtime could read.

GitAgent is that moment for agents.

Link to the GitHub repo in the comments.

Shubham Saboo introduces GitAgent, an open-source tool designed to solve AI agent framework fragmentation by providing a standardized folder structure that allows agents to be defined once and run across multiple frameworks (Claude Code, OpenAI, CrewAI, LangChain, Google Gemini, OpenClaw, Cursor) without rewriting code. The solution treats agent configuration as code using Git for version control and supervision, includes role-based access control for regulated industries, and enables agent composition through submodules.

directClaims: GitAgent is an open-source tool that allows defining agents once and running them across multiple frameworks, Current agent frameworks (CrewAI, LangChain, Claude Code, OpenAI, Google Gemini, OpenClaw) each have different definitions and formats, Switching between frameworks currently requires rewriting prompts, tools, memory, and config, GitAgent uses a standard folder structure with agent.yaml, SOUL.md, RULES.md, skills/, tools/, and memory/, GitAgent can export to Claude Code, OpenAI Agents SDK, CrewAI, LangGraph, Google Gemini CLI, OpenClaw, and Cursor, GitAgent includes built-in segregation of duties with maker, checker, and auditor roles for regulated industries, Sub-agents can be composed like submodules in GitAgent

inferredBeliefs: Current AI agent framework fragmentation is a significant problem in the ecosystem, Framework vendor lock-in is a barrier to agent adoption and portability, Treating agent configuration as code with Git version control improves agent quality and oversight, A standardized agent specification could unify the ecosystem like Docker did for containers, Agent supervision and governance are important for regulated industries

items.document:
NASA figured out how to write bulletproof code in 2006 and almost nobody used it.

Gerard Holzmann at JPL published the "Power of Ten," ten rules for safety-critical software. Simple stuff: every function fits on one printed page, every loop has a fixed upper bound, no dynamic memory after initialization, minimum two assertions per function. He kept it to ten rules because he'd watched organizations write 100+ page coding standards that developers just ignored.

The problem was always enforcement. You could put these rules on a wiki and tell people to follow them, but verifying compliance across hundreds of thousands of lines of code required either expensive tooling or a very patient human reviewer. Neither scaled well outside of JPL.

I think about this a lot, actually. Not just the Power of Ten specifically, but this whole category of things: ideas that were clearly right but too expensive to enforce. Checklists that worked in theory but fell apart in practice. The gap between knowing what good looks like and actually doing it consistently.

That gap is what AI agents collapse. You encode the rules, the agent applies them on every commit, every PR, every deployment. It doesn't have an opinion about whether the rules are worth following. It doesn't skip the check because it's been a long week.

I'm building agent workflows at Whimsey Labs right now, and honestly the hardest part isn't getting agents to follow rules. It's figuring out which rules are actually worth encoding. That's where the Power of Ten is helpful as a reference. Holzmann spent years figuring out which ten rules would give you the most coverage with the least friction. That kind of thinking transfers directly.

Still figuring out the velocity tradeoff. But the distance between "best practices exist" and "best practices get enforced" just shrank by a lot.

https://lnkd.in/gQ8txDXj

Michael Ulin discusses NASA's 'Power of Ten' coding rules from 2006 and why they weren't widely adopted, attributing the failure to enforcement challenges at scale. He argues that AI agents can now bridge the gap between knowing best practices and enforcing them consistently, while noting that the real challenge is determining which rules are worth encoding rather than getting agents to follow them.

directClaims: Gerard Holzmann at JPL published the 'Power of Ten' - ten rules for safety-critical software in 2006, The Power of Ten rules include: every function fits on one printed page, every loop has a fixed upper bound, no dynamic memory after initialization, minimum two assertions per function, Holzmann limited the rules to ten because organizations writing 100+ page coding standards were ignored by developers, Enforcement of coding standards at scale required expensive tooling or manual review, which didn't scale outside JPL, AI agents can collapse the gap between knowing best practices and enforcing them consistently, AI agents apply rules on every commit, PR, and deployment without bias or fatigue

inferredBeliefs: Most organizations fail to follow established best practices due to enforcement challenges, Simplicity and brevity in standards are more effective than comprehensive documentation, Automation through AI is more effective than human-dependent enforcement mechanisms, The hardest part of implementing agent workflows is deciding which rules to encode, not getting agents to follow rules, Best practices like the Power of Ten have lasting value and are applicable to modern AI-driven development

items.document:
After 2,000+ hours using Claude Code across real production codebases, I can tell you the thing that separates reliable from unreliable isn't the model, the prompt, or even the task complexity.

It's context management.

About 80% of the coding agent failures I see trace back to poor context - either too much noise, the wrong information loaded at the wrong time, or context that's drifted from the actual state of the codebase. Even with a 1M token window, Chroma's research shows that performance degrades as context grows. More tokens is not always better.

I built the WISC framework (inspired by Anthropic's research) to handle this systematically. 

Four strategy areas:

W - Write (externalize your agent's memory)
- Git log as long-term memory with standardized commit messages
- Plan in one session, implement in a fresh one
- Progress files and handoffs for cross-session state

I - Isolate (keep your main context clean)
- Subagents for research (90.2% improvement per Anthropic's data)
- Scout pattern to preview docs before committing them to main context

S - Select (just in time, not just in case)
- Global rules (always loaded)
- On-demand context for specific code areas
- Skills with progressive disclosure
- Prime commands for live codebase exploration

C - Compress (only when you have to)
- Handoffs for custom session summaries
- /compact with targeted summarization instructions

These work on any codebase. Not just greenfield side projects! I've applied this on enterprise codebases spanning multiple repositories, and the reliability improvement is consistent.

I also just published a YouTube video going over the WISC framework in a lot more detail. Very value packed! Check it out here:

https://lnkd.in/ggxxepik

Cole Medin shares insights from extensive Claude Code usage, arguing that context management—not model capability—is the primary determinant of coding agent reliability. He presents the WISC framework (Write, Isolate, Select, Compress) as a systematic approach to managing agent context across production codebases, supported by research citations and demonstrated improvements.

directClaims: After 2,000+ hours using Claude Code across real production codebases, context management separates reliable from unreliable systems, About 80% of coding agent failures trace back to poor context, Performance degrades as context grows even with a 1M token window, Subagents for research show 90.2% improvement per Anthropic's data, WISC framework works on any codebase including enterprise codebases spanning multiple repositories

items.document:
THIS is the wildest open-source project I’ve seen this month.

We were all hyped about Andrej Karpathy's autoresearch project automating the experiment loop a few weeks ago.
(ICYMI → https://lnkd.in/eDQBJQEz)

But a bunch of folks just took it ten steps further and automated the entire scientific method end-to-end.

It's called AutoResearchClaw, and it's fully open-source.

You pass it a single CLI command with a raw idea, and it completely takes over 🤯 

The 23-stage loop they designed is insane:

✦ First, it handles the literature review.
- It searches arXiv and Semantic Scholar for real papers 
- Cross-references them against DataCite and CrossRef. 
- No fake papers make it through.

✦ Second, it runs the sandbox.
- It generates the code from scratch.
- If the code breaks, it self-heals. 
- You don't have to step in.

✦ Finally, it writes the paper.
- It structures 5,000+ words into Introduction, Related Work, Method, and Experiments. 
- It formats the math, generates the comparison charts,
- Then wraps the whole thing in official ICML or ICLR LaTeX templates.

You can set it to pause for human approval, or you can just pass the --auto-approve flag and walk away.

What it spits out at the end:
→ Full academic paper draft
→ Conference-grade .tex files
→ Verified, hallucination-free citations
→ All experiment scripts and sandbox results

This is what autonomous AI agents actually look like in 2026.

Here is the repo:
→ https://lnkd.in/eX_VXkDZ

Massive kudos to the team for making this open-source 🤗 

_____
Built by → https://lnkd.in/eY5u8jsf

If you enjoyed this, feel free to repost to support their work ♻️

Don't forget to ⭐ the repo to boost visibility.

For more on AI agents and LLM workflows, follow me here or on 𝕏 → @datachaz

Users can submit a raw research idea via CLI and receive a complete paper draft, with optional human approval checkpoints or full automation via flags.

items.document:
Everyone's building AI agent demos.

Palantir shipped an end-to-end agentic architecture.

12 layers. The ENTIRE production stack. Here's what's inside:

1️⃣ Secure LLM integration
GPT, Gemini, Claude, Llama, Meta: commercial and open-source. Zero data retention by providers. Swap models without rebuilding.

2️⃣ Context Engineering
Real-time, pro-code and no-code. Contextual data, contextual logic, and systems of action feeding the Ontology.

3️⃣ The Ontology
Not a data lake. A digital twin with semantic, kinetic, and dynamic layers. Human+AI decision model built in. This is their moat.

4️⃣ Agent Lifecycle
Agent building, orchestration, and evaluation suites. All integrated. No stitching 5 tools together.

5️⃣ Security & Governance
Role-based, marking-based, purpose-based controls. System-wide branching. Approvals. Checkpoints. What regulated industries ACTUALLY need.

6️⃣ End-to-end Observability
Every tool call. Every data access. Every decision traced. Full model catalog.

Plus 6 more layers: tool services, multimodal compute, development environments, human+AI applications, package/release/deploy, and enterprise automation.

12 layers. One architecture. Production-ready.

The framework wars were the warmup.

The real game is agentic architecture.

Are you still debating agent frameworks? 👇

♻️ Repost for someone building agents who hasn't seen this architecture

Paolo Perrone highlights Palantir's 12-layer end-to-end agentic architecture as a production-ready solution that goes beyond typical AI agent demos. The architecture integrates secure LLM integration, context engineering, an ontology-based knowledge layer, agent lifecycle management, security and governance controls, and full observability. The post argues that enterprise agentic architecture, not framework selection, is now the key competitive battleground.

Last week my team held a one-hour context engineering sprint.

Massively valuable.

We blocked off everyone’s calendar for one hour, opened up Claude code, connected our context vault prompts and interview style, shared best practices ahead of time, and dictated back-and-forth with Claude for an hour (while walking for some of us!) with Wispr Flow.

Our file system is our agent brain. Taking an hour or two to massively improve that is well worth it.

Try it with your team next week.

NVIDIA just dropped a FREE 120B reasoning model.

Only 12B active during inference. 

450 tokens per second. 3x faster than Qwen3.5

Open-source reasoning models were supposed to be slow and expensive.

Here's why that argument just died:

1️⃣ Latent Mixture-of-Experts
Traditional models activate ALL parameters for every token.
Nemotron 3 Super routes to specialized experts.
120B knowledge base. 12B compute cost.

2️⃣ Hybrid Mamba-Transformer
Mamba layers handle long sequences in linear time.
Transformer layers handle complex reasoning.
Best of both worlds in one model.

3️⃣ Multi-Token Prediction
Standard LLMs predict one token at a time.
This predicts 3 tokens per step.
That's where the 450 TPS comes from.

But the feature I care about most:

You can turn reasoning ON and OFF.

◦ enable_thinking: True → deep reasoning chains for hard problems
◦ enable_thinking: False → fast direct responses for simple tasks
◦ low_effort: True → somewhere in between

One model. Three modes. You control the compute.

Open weights. Open datasets (10T curated tokens)

You can run it on consumer hardware (NVFP4 on DGX Spark).

The Nemotron 3 family:
1️⃣ Nano (30B-A3B) → available Dec. 2025
2️⃣ Super (120B-A12B) → available now! 
3️⃣ Ultra (~500B-A50B) → H1 2026

This is NVIDIA saying open-source doesn't have to be slow or expensive.

Which reasoning model are you running in production? 👇

♻️ Repost for someone still paying for slow inference.

items.document:
Garry Tan, CEO of Y Combinator, just shared his Claude Code setup.

4,000+ lines of code. Full tests. Under 1 hour.

Here's what makes his approach DIFFERENT:

He doesn't ask Claude to write code first.

He asks it to review the plan. Like a staff engineer would before any changes are made.

One prompt forces Claude through a 4-stage structured review:

1️⃣ Architecture
System design, component boundaries, dependency graphs, scaling risks, security holes.

2️⃣ Code Quality
DRY violations flagged aggressively. Error handling gaps. Technical debt hotspots. Over-engineered vs under-engineered areas called out.

3️⃣ Tests
Coverage gaps across unit, integration, e2e. Missing edge cases. Untested failure modes. "Too many tests > too few."

4️⃣ Performance
N+1 queries. Memory usage. Caching opportunities. High-complexity paths.

For EVERY issue found, Claude must:

⚡ Describe the problem with file and line references
⚡ Present 2-3 options including "do nothing"
⚡ Specify effort, risk, impact, and maintenance burden
⚡ Make an opinionated recommendation
⚡ Ask for approval before proceeding

Two modes built in:

BIG CHANGE: full review, max 4 issues per section
SMALL CHANGE: one focused question per section

His 5 engineering preferences baked into the prompt:

1️⃣ DRY is non-negotiable
2️⃣ Well-tested code is non-negotiable
3️⃣ "Engineered enough" — not hacky, not over-abstracted
4️⃣ Handle MORE edge cases, not fewer
5️⃣ Explicit over clever. Thoughtfulness over speed.

The real insight:

Review before implementation. Tradeoffs before code. Feedback before action.

For small teams without a senior engineer on every PR: design the review process into your AI.

Are you still letting Claude Code skip the review? 👇

♻️ Repost for someone shipping code without a structured review process

Paolo Perrone highlights Garry Tan's innovative Claude Code workflow that prioritizes structured review before implementation. The approach uses a 4-stage review framework (Architecture, Code Quality, Tests, Performance) where Claude analyzes code against 5 core engineering principles, presents options with tradeoffs, and requests approval before changes. This systematized review process is positioned as especially valuable for small teams lacking senior engineering oversight.

directClaims: Garry Tan, CEO of Y Combinator, shared his Claude Code setup, His approach uses a 4-stage structured review before code implementation, The review covers Architecture, Code Quality, Tests, and Performance, Claude must describe problems with file and line references for every issue, Claude presents 2-3 options including 'do nothing' for each issue, Two modes exist: BIG CHANGE (full review, max 4 issues per section) and SMALL CHANGE (one focused question per section), His 5 engineering preferences are: DRY is non-negotiable, well-tested code is non-negotiable, engineered enough (not hacky/over-abstracted), handle more edge cases, explicit over clever

inferredBeliefs: Review before implementation is more effective than implementation before review, AI-assisted code review should be structured with specific evaluation criteria, Small teams without senior engineers benefit from systematized AI-driven review processes, Engineering tradeoffs should be explicitly discussed before implementation, Opinionated recommendations from AI improve code quality decisions, Approval-seeking behavior in AI prevents unvetted changes

items.document:
Anthropic quietly showed one of the smartest ways to make Claude better over time.

It is not a new model.
It is one markdown file.

Boris Cherny, the creator of Claude Code, shared part of how their team works internally:

They use a CLAUDE.md file.
You write your rules once.
 Claude reads them before every session.
 Your workflow becomes consistent.
 Your corrections start compounding.

Here’s the setup:

1. For Claude Code
Create a file called CLAUDE.md
 Put it inside your project folder.
Claude uses it as persistent context for how you want work done.
That means you can tell it things like:
✦ Always plan before writing code
 ✦ For tasks with multiple steps, write a short spec first
 ✦ Run tests before marking anything complete
 ✦ Check logs before claiming a fix works
 ✦ Avoid quick hacks when a cleaner solution exists
 ✦ Document repeated mistakes so they do not happen again

This is where it gets powerful:
 Week 1: you correct Claude a few times
 Week 4: it starts following your workflow naturally
 Week 12: it feels less like a tool and more like a real teammate

2. For your personal workflow
You can do something similar with a profile.md file.
Use it to store:
✦ Your name, role, and priorities
 ✦ Your tone and writing preferences
 ✦ Your preferred output formats
 ✦ Things the AI should avoid
 ✦ Your active projects and current context

Instead of repeating yourself in every session,
 you give the AI a permanent operating manual.


That is the real lesson here:
The best AI users are not just prompting better.
They are building systems around the model.
One file will not make Claude magical overnight.

But one good context file, improved over time, can completely change how useful it becomes.

That is how AI stops feeling random
 and starts feeling operational.

Anthropic internally uses CLAUDE.md files as persistent context to improve Claude's consistency and workflow adherence. By documenting rules, preferences, and corrections once and letting Claude reference them each session, users experience compounding improvements over weeks and months, transforming Claude from a tool into a reliable teammate. This approach demonstrates that the best AI usage involves building systematic frameworks around models rather than relying on better prompting alone.

directClaims: Anthropic uses a CLAUDE.md file internally to improve Claude's consistency, A CLAUDE.md file placed in a project folder acts as persistent context for Claude, Claude reads the CLAUDE.md file before every session, User corrections compound over time when using a context file, After 4 weeks of use, Claude starts following the workflow naturally, After 12 weeks, the tool feels like a real teammate rather than just a tool

inferredBeliefs: Persistent context files are more effective than repeated prompting, AI improvement comes from systematic approaches rather than ad-hoc prompting, Documenting mistakes prevents their recurrence in AI interactions, The best AI users build systems around models rather than just prompt better, AI usefulness increases dramatically when paired with well-maintained documentation

There is a reason why Algorithms and Data Structures are hard for many software engineers.

It's not because they're not capable, but they simply miss a lot of pre-requisite knowledge.

If you want to learn dsa from first principles, read these 16 articles (links below):

1. How depth-first search shines on directed graphs: topological sorting and strongly connected component algorithms
https://lnkd.in/eSw2mnjh
2. Depth First Search and its applications on undirected graphs: finding cycles and articulation vertices.
https://lnkd.in/eShkbN72
3. Breadth First Search and its applications: shortest paths, connected components and bipartite testing.
https://lnkd.in/eZkHfhvN
4. A beginner friendly introduction to graphs. Different types of graphs and how to represent them.
https://lnkd.in/eUwk5Y9v
5. What is a priority queue and how to implement it. Plus priority queues use cases and the heapsort algorithm.
https://lnkd.in/eztWMfwN
6. Three must to know applications of the heap data structures: largest elements, graphs, and Huffmann encoding.
https://lnkd.in/enfeNM-N
7. From traversal to reversal: essential linked list algorithms and techniques to solve common interview problems.
https://lnkd.in/eYruabNt
8. How to quickly calculate range queries on arrays using prefix sum and sparse tables.
https://lnkd.in/emgb2dA6
9. How to use the two-pointer method to efficiently solving array and string problems (with code templates).
https://lnkd.in/eaxNapz4
10. How to effectively use sets and hash tables to solve coding interview problems.
https://lnkd.in/e6j4gp2G
11. How to master backtracking algorithms: a step by step guide to solve any problem.
https://lnkd.in/edwbW5uv

12. Why comparison based algorithms cannot sort in less than O(nlogn). And why counting sort, radix sort and bucket sort can do better.
https://lnkd.in/eTpk4rfB
13. How to efficiently store and represent strings using a trie data structure.
https://lnkd.in/edNtV2yK
14. How to analyze the space complexity of an algorithm: time and space trade-offs and recursion.
https://lnkd.in/eEB-e2P3
15. A 7 steps bulletproof framework to ace your next technical interview round.
https://lnkd.in/ecEF-W3b

items.document:
Our new course, "Agentic AI Engineering," contains 17 notebooks and a complete multi-agent project (because you can't learn to build production agents through notebooks only).

Notebooks are really powerful for teaching the core concepts quickly and easily to anyone. Which we can then use in the final project. You don't have to set up your environment (using Colab), or you can do so very easily with specific requirement packages. 

Still, we also believe it's important to "sweat" in order to learn, and notebooks are thus not the entirety of the course. The main goal of the course is to build your own production-grade agent systems (deployed to the cloud with Docker and Cloud Run, managed with CI/CD) by leveraging the techniques we teach through notebooks.

We teach every core technique in those notebooks. Each one has a dedicated course lesson, which serves a specific purpose through the applied project.

We keep it super practical by teaching throughout the course to replicate our very own Research Agent and Writing Workflow, giving all the techniques we've ever tried (including some we didn't keep in the final version but that can be relevant). 

Then, the student's goal is to build and deploy their own MCP server, implementing the most advanced agent patterns, evaluation systems, and observability techniques, which becomes an amazing portfolio project.

If, after reading this, you feel it might be a course for you, check it out here: https://lnkd.in/eBCqSjMc
And the (free) notebooks here: https://lnkd.in/ecN7kMvK

items.document:
Most people use Claude Code like a chatbot.

Describe what you want, get some code, fix the bits that are wrong, repeat. That works for small stuff. It falls apart on anything complex.

I use five slash commands that turn Claude Code into a structured engineering workflow.

/brainstorm is where I develop an idea. It explores the codebase, asks me questions, and proposes approaches with trade-offs. If the feature needs multiple tasks it writes a sprint with dependencies, so each task can be specced individually.

/spec-task produces functional requirements through iterative conversation. What to build, not how. The output is a self-contained document a completely fresh session can pick up and understand.

/plan-task consults expert subagents that research online documentation, API specs, local repos for dependent libraries, and examples I've built before. Then it writes a phased implementation plan with testing strategy and anti-patterns to avoid.

/implement-task executes phase by phase. At every phase boundary it updates the task docs, runs tests, and commits. When the context compacts (and it will), the next session reads those docs and picks up exactly where it left off.

/review-task audits the code against the spec. Mechanical check first, then expert subagent validation of every issue found.

None of this is radical. It's just software engineering. Specs before code. Plans before implementation. Reviews before merge. The only difference is Claude Code does most of the legwork.

John George describes a five-command structured workflow for using Claude Code effectively on complex projects. Rather than treating it as a chatbot, he implements traditional software engineering practices: brainstorming with trade-off analysis, writing specifications, planning with subagents, phased implementation with testing, and code review. This approach allows context to be managed across sessions through documentation and commits.

directClaims: Most people use Claude Code like a chatbot for small tasks, The chatbot approach falls apart on complex projects, Five slash commands can turn Claude Code into a structured engineering workflow, /brainstorm explores the codebase, asks questions, and proposes approaches with trade-offs, /spec-task produces functional requirements through iterative conversation, /plan-task consults expert subagents to research documentation and write implementation plans, /implement-task executes phase by phase with updates, tests, and commits, /review-task audits code against specs with mechanical checks and expert validation, Claude Code can handle traditional software engineering practices like specs, plans, and reviews

items.document:
Most think Claude Code = terminal + prompt.

That barely scratches the surface.

The real power of Claude Code isn’t the prompt.
It’s the system around the prompt.

Once you understand this, everything changes.

Claude Code actually works like a 4-layer AI engineering system:

1️⃣ CLAUDE.md → Project memory
Persistent context for your project.
Stores rules, conventions, and architecture decisions.

2️⃣ Skills → Reusable knowledge packs
Claude automatically invokes them when relevant.
Think: reusable workflows and expertise.

3️⃣ Hooks → Deterministic guardrails
Safety gates that run automatically.
Unlike prompts, hooks are 100% enforced.

4️⃣ Agents → Specialized sub-agents
Each with their own context window.
Great for complex, multi-step tasks.

Most engineers install Claude Code and immediately start prompting.

But the difference between average output and production-level results usually comes down to setup.

Practices that make a huge difference:

→ Run /init on day one to generate CLAUDE.md
→ Organize the .claude/ directory (skills, hooks, permissions)
→ Write clear skill descriptions so Claude knows when to trigger them
→ Use the memory hierarchy (global → project → folder)
→ Add hooks for critical rules (hooks = enforced, memory = advisory)

When structured properly, Claude Code stops feeling like a chatbot.

It starts behaving like a real AI development system.

If you want to learn it properly, Anthropic released several free courses:

Claude 101
https://lnkd.in/g6PiBcg5

AI Fluency: Framework & Foundations
https://lnkd.in/gEkvdXHX

AI Fluency for Students
https://lnkd.in/gwPuhg2k

AI Fluency for Educators
https://lnkd.in/gwPuhg2k

Teaching AI Fluency
https://lnkd.in/grzJmi8n

Also includes developer topics like:

• Claude Code in Action
• Building with the Claude API
• Prompt Engineering tutorials
• Model Context Protocol (MCP) fundamentals

Bookmark this if you're building with Claude.

The future of AI development isn't just prompts.

It's systems around prompts.

Divyanshi Sharma explains that Claude Code's power lies not in simple prompting but in its 4-layer system architecture: CLAUDE.md for project memory, Skills as reusable knowledge packs, Hooks as enforced guardrails, and Agents for complex tasks. She advocates that proper setup and organization are critical for production-level results, recommends specific initialization practices, and directs readers to Anthropic's free educational courses on Claude development.

items.document:
Someone finally documented how to actually use Claude Code.

13K stars. claude-code-best-practice.

Direct from Boris Cherny and team:

→ Always use plan mode, give Claude a way to verify
→ Ask Claude to interview you using AskUserQuestion tool
→ Use Git Worktrees for parallel development
→ /loop - schedule recurring tasks for up to 3 days
→ Code Review - fresh context windows catch bugs the original agent missed
→ /btw - side chain conversations while Claude works
→ Make phase-wise gated plans with tests for each phase
→ Use cross-model (Claude Code + Codex) to review your plan
→ CLAUDE[.]md should target under 200 lines per file
→ Use commands for workflows instead of sub-agents
→ Have feature-specific sub-agents with skills instead of general QA or backend engineer
→ Vanilla Claude Code is better than complex workflows for smaller tasks
→ Take screenshots and share with Claude when stuck
→ Use MCP to let Claude see Chrome console logs
→ Ask Claude to run terminal as background task for better debugging
→ Use cross-model for QA - e.g. Codex for plan and implementation review

The community workflows included:
→ Cross-Model (Claude Code + Codex) Workflow
→ RPI (Research Plan Implement)
→ Ralph Wiggum Loop for autonomous tasks
→ Github Speckit (74K stars)
→ obra/superpowers (72K stars)
→ OpenSpec OPSX (28K stars)

The billion-dollar questions it addresses:
→ What should you put inside CLAUDE[.]md?
→ When should you use command vs agent vs skill?
→ Why does Claude ignore CLAUDE[.]md instructions?
→ Can we convert a codebase into specs and regenerate code from those specs alone?

The daily habits:
→ Update Claude Code daily
→ Start your day by reading the changelog
→ Follow r/ClaudeAI, r/ClaudeCode on Reddit


GitHub Repo: https://lnkd.in/din-wP8h

items.document:
Google just gave every AI coding assistant NATIVE knowledge of how to build agents.

15,600 GitHub stars. One command. FREE.

Your coding assistant doesn't know ADK. It hallucinates APIs and invents methods that don't exist.

Here's why this changes everything:

npx skills add google/adk-docs/skills -y

One command. Your coding agent now knows:

1️⃣ ADK Cheatsheet
Full Python API reference. Every method, every parameter. No more hallucinated imports.

2️⃣ ADK Dev Guide
Development lifecycle and coding guidelines. The patterns Google's own team uses.

5️⃣ ADK Observability Guide
Tracing, logging, and integrations. See what your agents are ACTUALLY doing.

6️⃣ ADK Scaffold
New agent project in seconds. Right structure from the start.

Works with everything:

⚡ Gemini CLI: extension install
⚡ Any MCP-compatible tool

Plus a docs MCP server that connects your editor directly to ADK documentation. Always up to date. Machine-readable.

What this replaces:

Hours of Googling API docs → $0
Debugging hallucinated methods → $0
Manually reading changelogs → $0

ADK has 15,600 stars, 2,800+ dependent projects, and now ships in Python, TypeScript, Go, and Java.

Google isn't just building an agent framework.

They're teaching YOUR coding assistant how to use it.

🔗 Install skills: npx skills add google/adk-docs/skills -y
🔗 Full docs: google.github.io/adk-docs

Are you still letting your coding assistant hallucinate ADK APIs? 👇

items.document:
This tool analyzes your hardware with one command and shows which LLMs are actually worth running on your machine 👇

It's called llmfit - and it solves the most annoying part of running local AI.

Instead of downloading a model and hitting an OOM error, it scans your RAM, CPU, GPU, and VRAM first - then scores every model across 4 dimensions:

1. Quality - param count, model family, quantization penalty
2. Speed - estimated tok/s for your exact backend (CUDA, Metal, ROCm)
3. Fit - memory utilization vs. your available hardware
4. Context - context window vs. your use case

Each model gets a label: Perfect / Good / Marginal / Too Tight.

It also picks the best quantization automatically - starting at Q8_0, stepping down to Q2_K until something fits.

The detail I like most: it handles MoE architectures correctly. Mixtral 8x7B has 46.7B total params, but only ~12.9B are active per token. llmfit accounts for this, while many tools still miscalculate it.

Covers hundreds of models across major providers including Meta, Mistral, Qwen, DeepSeek, and more. Ollama integration built in.

14k+ stars. 100% open source. 
Link to the repo: https://lnkd.in/gpWPVS7g

-- 
P.S. We’re building the best AI English tutor in the world. Try it to improve your speaking, it’s 15x cheaper the human one → GetFluently.app

Stanislav Beliaev presents llmfit, an open source tool that analyzes hardware capabilities and recommends compatible LLMs before installation. It scores models on Quality, Speed, Fit, and Context, automatically selects optimal quantization, and correctly handles MoE architectures. The tool integrates with Ollama and covers models from major providers.

directClaims: llmfit analyzes hardware with one command and shows which LLMs are worth running on your machine, It scans RAM, CPU, GPU, and VRAM before downloading models, It scores models across 4 dimensions: Quality, Speed, Fit, and Context, It picks the best quantization automatically, starting at Q8_0 and stepping down to Q2_K, It handles MoE architectures correctly, Mixtral 8x7B has 46.7B total params but only ~12.9B are active per token, It covers hundreds of models across Meta, Mistral, Qwen, DeepSeek, and more, It has Ollama integration built in, It has 14k+ stars and is 100% open source

items.document:
I just deleted all my MCPs.
Skills + CLI is all you need.

MCPs were a solution for humans pretending to build for agents. But agents aren't humans. They're processes. And processes talk to the world through CLIs and APIs. Always have. Always will.

GitHub's official MCP server: 54,000 tokens to load the spec. Fifty-four thousand. Before I even ask it to do anything. 
Meanwhile, `gh --help` costs 562 tokens. And the model already knows it from training data.

MCP is a JSON bureaucracy pretending to be infrastructure. It's the enterprise middleware of AI, lots of ceremony, zero value add. 

Skills are 30-50 tokens until triggered. Progressive disclosure. They teach the agent how to think. CLIs are the what- battle-tested, version-controlled, debuggable without a spec document.

Make sure you own your AI. AI in the cloud is not aligned with you; it’s aligned with the company that owns it.

Mitko Vasilev argues against using Model Context Protocol (MCP) for AI agents, advocating instead for traditional CLIs and APIs combined with lightweight Skills. He critiques MCPs for excessive token overhead, unnecessary complexity, and enterprise bureaucracy, while emphasizing the importance of owning and controlling AI systems rather than relying on cloud-based solutions.

directClaims: MCPs were a solution for humans pretending to build for agents, Agents are processes that talk to the world through CLIs and APIs, GitHub's official MCP server requires 54,000 tokens to load the spec, gh --help costs 562 tokens, MCP is a JSON bureaucracy pretending to be infrastructure, Skills are 30-50 tokens until triggered, AI in the cloud is not aligned with you; it's aligned with the company that owns it

inferredBeliefs: Token efficiency is a critical metric for agent tool integration, Complexity and overhead in tooling frameworks reduce their practical value, Established CLIs and APIs are more suitable for agent interaction than purpose-built protocols, Simplicity and progressive disclosure are preferable to comprehensive specifications, Data ownership and control over AI systems is important, Enterprise middleware patterns are being inappropriately applied to AI tooling

items.document:
All legacy codebase modernization efforts in 2026 begin with: “let’s give an agent the repos”
Which is a great way to let Claude Code, opencode, Copilot, and friends misunderstand 40 repositories at machine speed.

In real enterprise code estates, the architecture usually lives in:
one staff engineer’s head… who retired in 2024,
three geological eras of DI,
`HttpClient` chains crossing repo boundaries,
and EF Core mappings to tables named by ancient forces.

My agents use local AI with dede, an engineering R&D context system, short for `DogEatDog.DependencyExplorer`

It scans a workspace full of sibling repos and extracts a deterministic Roslyn-backed AST/semantic graph across controllers, services, methods, internal HTTP calls, EF Core lineage, and cross-repo dependencies.

Here is a live scan over 13 repos, 1,962 projects, and 52,800 methods.
Not regex theater. 
Not architecture fan fiction. 
Not LLM vibes.
Just 100% local AI and tooling.

The useful part is giving that graph thingy to AI coding agents as a skill.
Now agents can query compiler-backed structure instead of improvising:
“What breaks if I change this endpoint?” 
“Which tables are in the blast radius?” 
“Where does this service cross repo boundaries?” 
“Which edges are ambiguous and need a human?”

Make sure you own your AI. AI in the cloud is not aligned with you; it’s aligned with the company that owns it.

Mitko Vasilev argues that naive approaches to legacy codebase modernization using generic AI agents will fail at enterprise scale. He presents dede (DogEatDog.DependencyExplorer), a local AI system that uses Roslyn-backed static analysis to create deterministic semantic graphs of complex multi-repository codebases. This context enables AI agents to make informed architectural decisions by querying compiler-backed structure rather than improvising. He emphasizes the importance of local AI ownership over cloud-based solutions for proper alignment with organizational goals.

directClaims: Legacy codebase modernization efforts in 2026 will begin with giving agents access to repositories, Claude Code, Copilot, and similar tools misunderstand multiple repositories at scale, Enterprise code architecture often lives in individual staff engineers' heads, Real enterprise codebases have multiple dependency injection implementations across geological time periods, HttpClient chains cross repository boundaries in enterprise systems, EF Core mappings exist to tables with non-standard naming conventions, dede (DogEatDog.DependencyExplorer) scans workspaces and extracts deterministic AST/semantic graphs, The tool processes 13 repos, 1,962 projects, and 52,800 methods, AI agents can query compiler-backed structure instead of improvising, Cloud-based AI is aligned with the company that owns it, not with the user

inferredBeliefs: Current AI agents struggle with understanding complex legacy enterprise codebases, Local AI tooling with proper context is superior to generic cloud-based AI solutions, Deterministic, compiler-based analysis is more reliable than regex-based or heuristic approaches, Enterprise architecture documentation is often incomplete or non-existent, AI agents need structured dependency and relationship data to make sound architectural decisions, Ownership and control of AI tools is important for alignment with organizational goals

LangChain just open-sourced their answer to Claude Code.

9.9K GitHub stars. 5 hours old.

Deep Agents: the batteries-included agent harness. MIT licensed. FREE.

Here's why this is a big deal:

Claude Code is amazing but closed-source and locked to Anthropic.

Deep Agents gives you the same architecture. Any model. Any provider.

What's included out of the box:

1️⃣ Planning
write_todos for task breakdown and progress tracking. The agent plans before it codes.

2️⃣ Filesystem access
read_file, write_file, edit_file, ls, glob, grep. Full codebase navigation.

3️⃣ Shell execution
Run commands with sandboxing. Build, test, deploy from the agent.

4️⃣ Sub-agents
Spawn isolated workers with their own context windows. Parallel work without context pollution.

5️⃣ Context management
Auto-summarization when conversations get long. Large outputs saved to files. The problem Claude Code solved, now OPEN SOURCE.

Two lines to start:

pip install deepagents

agent = create_deep_agent()

Works with Claude, OpenAI, Google, or any LangChain-compatible model. Swap providers without rebuilding.

Built on LangGraph. Streaming, persistence, checkpointing: all production-ready.

The CLI adds web search, remote sandboxes, persistent memory, custom skills, and human-in-the-loop approval.

From their README: "This project was primarily inspired by Claude Code."

They're not hiding it. They're DEMOCRATIZING it.

Are you still locked into a single AI coding provider? 👇

♻️ Repost for someone who wants Claude Code capabilities without the lock-in

items.document:
AI Feature vs AI-Native Platform: They are NOT the same.

If you’re unclear, read this:

The difference is about where the work happens: outside the product (suggestions) vs inside the workflow (actions).

That’s the shift happening right now:

AI that answers → AI that acts inside the platform.

Here's a simple breakdown (save this):

𝗖𝗵𝗮𝘁𝗯𝗼𝘁 𝗹𝗮𝘆𝗲𝗿 (𝗼𝗹𝗱 𝗳𝗲𝗮𝘁𝘂𝗿𝗲 𝗺𝗼𝗱𝗲𝗹)
 ↳ AI gives suggestions
 ↳ You interpret them
 ↳ You execute everything manually
 ↳ The software 𝗶𝗻𝗳𝗼𝗿𝗺𝘀 you. You do the work.

𝗘𝘅𝗲𝗰𝘂𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝗹𝗮𝘆𝗲𝗿 (𝗔𝗜-𝗻𝗮𝘁𝗶𝘃𝗲 𝗽𝗹𝗮𝘁𝗳𝗼𝗿𝗺)
 ↳ You describe the outcome
 ↳ The platform turns intent → actions
 ↳ It pulls context from your data + history
 ↳ It runs analysis, drafts changes, queues tasks
 ↳ You review, refine, approve
 ↳ The software 𝘄𝗼𝗿𝗸𝘀 𝘄𝗶𝘁𝗵 𝘆𝗼𝘂

Take Shopify Sidekick for example:
It’s not just a chatbot sitting on top of Shopify.
It’s 𝗲𝗺𝗯𝗲𝗱𝗱𝗲𝗱 𝗱𝗶𝗿𝗲𝗰𝘁𝗹𝘆 into the product layer.

Meaning it can take action inside Shopify itself, using your store’s context, permissions, and history, rather than generating suggestions you have to implement yourself.

𝗦𝗼 𝘄𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝘀𝗲𝗽𝗮𝗿𝗮𝘁𝗲𝘀 𝗮 𝗿𝗲𝗮𝗹 𝗽𝗹𝗮𝘁𝗳𝗼𝗿𝗺 𝘀𝗵𝗶𝗳𝘁 𝗳𝗿𝗼𝗺 𝗮 𝗳𝗲𝗮𝘁𝘂𝗿𝗲 𝗰𝗵𝗲𝗰𝗸𝗹𝗶𝘀𝘁?

Not “who has AI.”

But who can do these 4 things reliably:
1. Context → deep understanding of your business + current state
2. Trust → scoped permissions, previews, audit logs, rollback
3. Execution → multi-step tasks inside the real workflow (no context switching)
4. Memory → learns from outcomes, not just prompts

For example Sidekick can:
↳ Complete multi-step tasks like updating product collections, adjusting discounts, or configuring settings, without pulling you into separate dashboards.
↳ Turn plain English into a working custom app, generating the underlying code so merchants can build tools without coding.
↳ Generate analytics reports from natural-language questions, pulling directly from Shopify Analytics.
↳ Proactively surface weekly opportunities based on store performance, not just react when asked.
↳ Edit product images directly in the Shopify app; removing backgrounds, adjusting visuals, and publishing changes in place.

That’s not AI as an add-on.
That’s AI as an execution layer.

We’re moving toward 𝗱𝗲𝗰𝗶𝘀𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝗢𝗦 𝗽𝗹𝗮𝘁𝗳𝗼𝗿𝗺𝘀:
Intent → plan → action → feedback → memory (with guardrails).

If you’re building software, the question isn’t “Where do we add AI?”

It’s: What should this product safely do on the user’s behalf?

What else would you add?

→ https://lnkd.in/gHEunh4Y

--
📌 Save for later.
♻️ Repost to help other engineers learn and grow.
➕ Follow Shopify + thanks for sponsoring.

Nikki Siapno distinguishes between AI features and AI-native platforms, arguing that true platform shifts move AI from suggestion layers to execution layers embedded in workflows. Using Shopify Sidekick as an example, the post outlines four requirements for real AI platforms: context awareness, trust mechanisms, multi-step execution, and memory. The post advocates for thinking about AI as an execution layer rather than an add-on feature, moving toward 'decision OS' platforms that handle intent-to-action workflows with appropriate guardrails.

directClaims: AI features and AI-native platforms are not the same, The difference is about where the work happens: outside the product (suggestions) vs inside the workflow (actions), The shift is from AI that answers to AI that acts inside the platform, Chatbot layer model: AI gives suggestions, users interpret and execute manually, Execution layer model: users describe outcome, platform turns intent into actions, Shopify Sidekick is embedded directly into the product layer, not just a chatbot on top, Real platform shifts require: context, trust, execution, and memory, Sidekick can complete multi-step tasks like updating product collections and adjusting discounts without context switching, Sidekick can turn plain English into working custom apps with generated code, Sidekick can generate analytics reports from natural-language questions, Sidekick can proactively surface weekly opportunities based on store performance, Sidekick can edit product images directly in the Shopify app

inferredBeliefs: AI-native platforms provide more value than AI features by reducing manual work, The future of software involves decision OS platforms with intent-to-action workflows, Integration of AI into core workflows is more effective than additive chatbot layers, Trust and guardrails are essential for AI systems acting on user behalf, Context awareness and memory are critical differentiators for AI platforms, The question for product builders should be about safety and user benefit, not just AI adoption

Your AI governance is in a PDF.
Your agents are in production.

That is the gap.

8 layers. 30 runtime controls. 8 frameworks enforced at the architecture level.

NIST GOV 1.4 at the Policy Engine.
OWASP ASI10 at the Kill Switch.
GDPR Art.5(1) at Agent Memory.
SEC 17a-4 at the Audit Trail.

Every control fires at runtime. Every control produces evidence. No manual compliance. No spreadsheet governance.

4 trust zones. MCP-secured tool access. WORM audit logs. Kill switch under 5 seconds.

Full architecture below.

Built, not theorized.

items.document:
A candidate was asked this question in an LLM System Design Interview: "How would you reduce inference costs at scale?"
The answer always comes back to the same bottleneck, KV Cache and most people can't explain it clearly.

When an LLM generates a response, it computes attention over every prior token.

To avoid redoing that work on every step, it stores Key and Value vectors for each token in GPU memory.
That's the KV Cache.

The cache includes everything in context: system prompt, conversation history, your input, and output tokens generated so far.

A 128K context holds 32x more K/V pairs than a 4K one, 32x more GPU memory. Providers charge for that.

It's not just cost it's also speed. The initial prefill reads your full prompt once.
But every generated token attends over the full cache.
Longer context = slower generation, not just a bigger bill.

What to do:
→ Trim conversation history aggressively
→ Use retrieval instead of stuffing full docs into the prompt
→ Match your context window to what the task actually needs

The model isn't getting smarter with more context. It's getting more expensive.

You Switched from ChatGPT to Claude… Now What?

🤔 Are you using Claude like ChatGPT… or are you using it like a system?
Most people migrate tools.

 Very few upgrade their workflow.
Claude isn’t just “another chatbot.”
 It’s multiple execution layers — and each one serves a different purpose.

Here’s how to actually use it strategically 👇

🧠 1️⃣ Chat → For Thinking & Clarity
Use this when you need:
 ✔ Deep reasoning
 ✔ Structured writing
 ✔ Research synthesis
 ✔ Strategy breakdown

Tips:
 ▪ Use the most advanced model available to you
 ▪ Turn on extended reasoning when working on complex problems
 ▪ Treat it as a thinking partner — not a task executor

📂 2️⃣ Projects → Context Isolation

Best for:
 ✔ Client-based work
 ✔ Topic separation
 ✔ Focused knowledge environments

Drop files directly into each Project so Claude stays context-aware.
Think of Projects as clean workspaces.

💻 3️⃣ Code → For Developers

If you build software:
 ✔ It reads your codebase
 ✔ Suggests edits
 ✔ Helps debug
 ✔ Accelerates development
If you don’t code — skip this.
 Use execution layers instead.

🏢 4️⃣ Cowork → Operational Execution

This is where non-technical leverage happens.

Use it for:
 ✔ File organization
 ✔ Cross-document analysis
 ✔ Report generation
 ✔ Repetitive digital tasks

Pro Tip:
 Structure your workspace clearly (folders for profile info, templates, active projects, outputs).

Claude works best when your digital environment is organized.

🧩 5️⃣ Skills → Reusable Intelligence

If you repeat a task weekly, build a Skill.
✔ Permanent instructions
 ✔ Standardized workflows
 ✔ Consistent output
A strong Skill replaces long SOP documents.

🔗 6️⃣ Connectors → Claude Inside Your Tools
Integrate with:
 ✔ Slack
 ✔ Google Drive
 ✔ Notion
 ✔ Figma
 ✔ Other business tools
Now Claude doesn’t just generate answers.
 It works inside your ecosystem.

⚙️ 7️⃣ Plugins → Role-Based Acceleration
✔ Pre-built workflows
 ✔ Trigger commands
 ✔ Customize for your team

This turns Claude into a functional co-worker.
🔥 The Real Shift
If you’re only using Chat…
 you’re using 20% of Claude’s potential.

The leverage comes from:
 Thinking Layer + Execution Layer + Tool Integration
That’s when AI stops being a chatbot
 and starts becoming infrastructure.
Are you using Claude as a thinking assistant…
 or as an operational system?

This repo teaches AI Agents how to THINK.

Agent Skills for Context Engineering: the open-source skill library that every Claude Code and Cursor user needs.

Here's why context engineering > prompt engineering:

Prompt engineering: "how do I phrase this better?"
Context engineering: "what information does the AI need, and how do I structure it?"

One improves a single message. The other improves EVERY message.

This repo has 14 skills across 5 categories:

🔹 Foundations
1️⃣ Context Fundamentals: what context is, why it matters, anatomy of agent systems
2️⃣ Context Degradation: lost-in-middle, poisoning, distraction, clash
3️⃣ Context Compression: strategies for long-running sessions
4️⃣ Context Optimization: compaction, masking, caching

🔹 Architecture
5️⃣ Multi-Agent Patterns: orchestrator, peer-to-peer, hierarchical
6️⃣ Memory Systems: short-term, long-term, graph-based
7️⃣ Tool Design: building tools agents can actually use
8️⃣ Filesystem Context: dynamic discovery, plan persistence
9️⃣ Hosted Agents: background coding agents with sandboxed VMs

🔹 Evaluation
🔟 Evaluation Frameworks: test, measure, improve
⚡ Advanced Evaluation: LLM-as-judge, pairwise comparison, bias mitigation

🔹 Development
⚡ Project Development: ideation to deployment, task-model fit

🔹 Cognitive Architecture
⚡ BDI Mental States: beliefs, desires, intentions for rational agents

Install in Claude Code with one command:

/plugin marketplace add muratcankoylan/Agent-Skills-for-Context-Engineering
